content:
Hint:
If you look carefully, you will notice that the problem is actually asking for an arrangement of dominoes on the board that satisfies a particular property - no dominoes share a side or overlap.

To solve this problem, let's consider how many dominoes can fit on the board while ensuring that they do not share any sides (i.e., they only touch at vertices. This means we want the arrangement to have the most coverage with the least overlap. We need to cover as much area as possible with as few dominoes as possible. The best arrangement would be one where all dominoes form a checkerboard pattern.

If the board is a $2\times2$ square, it can hold 1 domino. A $3\times 3$ square can hold 2 dominoes, and a $4\times4$ can hold 6 dominoes. A $5\times5$ can hold 12 dominoes. So, if we can find the general formula for an $n\times n$ board, then we can apply it to our specific case of a $1000\times1000$ board.
